Of a system. It's an idealized representation. It's not perfectly reconstructing some system. It's sort of good enough for our purposes. It's not exactly how gravity works, but it's sort of an idealized approximate description.

So very famous quote that you'll hear a lot, and it's also a quote that's misused a lot. But George Bach said essentially all models are wrong, but some are useful. And this is something we're going to repeat throughout the class. Every model you work with for the most part, will not perfectly reconstruct your system. They're incorrect, they're wrong. But if you use them in the right context and use them in a smart way, they can be very useful for thinking about the world.

And So what are some 3 reasons you might you might build models, and we'll talk about all three of these in Data 100. The first one is inference. There's that word inference again from the last lecture. We talked about inference in the last lecture, meaning saying something meaningful about a population based on some information from a sample. So inference is just this idea of making sense of some phenomenon in the world. How do two things relate to one another? What is the correlation of two things?
The idea here is we want interpretable models. We don't want really fancy models that are hard to understand that are black.

Box like a large language model where we say we don't really know how it works but it outputs something. We actually want to understand how these kinds of models work. So sometimes when we're modeling, we want interpretable models and we want to try to get the simplest model. A word for this simple and interpretable as simple as possible is a parsimonious model, a model that explains as much as possible with as simple of a structure as possible. Prediction is another reason you might want to construct a model. So on the left inference that's very much like research understands.

In the world policy prediction very much like make money company, you also use it in policy in other places. But prediction is is trying to make an accurate prediction about the future. When we focus on a prediction task, we want our outcomes to be as accurate as possible from our model.
But we don't really care where we're coming from. It's OK for our model to be black box. Now, do we always want interpretable models or black?

Box models that predict really well, no, sometimes we want a little bit of both. But these two ideas are important to have in your head. This idea of inference and prediction. OK. There's also a third reason you might want to build a model, and that is to assess causality.

To assess causality, something we won't dig into a lot in Data 100, but will bring up every so often this sort of fundamental idea.
